coredata.dc:title: Single-well colorimetric sensor array for discrimination and smartphone-assisted detection of catecholamines based on Fe-carbon dots nanozymes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Guang-Ming
Chen, Dan-Dan
Xia, Yi-Fan
Cai, Zhi-Qiang
Cui, Shun-Qiang
Wei, Xia
Dou, Zhen-Chong
Yuan, Yuan
Sandra, Akimana
Yuan, Hou-Qun
coredata.dc:description:
Background Catecholamines (CAs), such as noradrenaline (NE), adrenaline (AD), and dopamine (DA), are essential signaling mediators that regulate various physiological functions. Monitoring their levels is crucial for studying and diagnosing diseases, as abnormal concentrations are associated with numerous health conditions. However, distinguishing between these CAs is challenging due to their highly similar molecular structures.
Results In this study, Fe-doped carbon dot-based nanozymes (Fe-CDs) with strong peroxidase-like activity were synthesized using a simple one-pot method. Fe-CDs-based sensing systems exhibit excellent stability, reproducibility, sensitivity (with detection limits of 26.6 nM for NE, 46.0 nM for AD, and 33.3 nM for DA), and anti-interference properties. A triple-channel single-well colorimetric sensor array was developed by collecting the absorbance at 20, 40, and 60 min as sensing units, enabling the effective differentiation and identification of various CAs.
Significance The Fe-CDs-based system has proven capable of detecting CAs in real human urine and fetal bovine serum. Additionally, the Fe-CDs-based smartphone-assisted platform provides efficient, highly sensitive, and on-site CAs detection, making it highly promising for biomedical and diagnostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Catecholamines
Smartphone-assisted detection
Colorimetric sensor array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assembling a triple-channel single-well sensor array
Construction of Fe-CDs colorimetric sensor array
Reproducibility and stability of sensing platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22166018, 22165012, 31960720), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei University of Technology (No. XJ2022007801), and the Open Project Funding of the Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education) (No. 202409FE20).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Fe-CDs (inset: the distribution of particle size.). (b) 3D AFM map of Fe-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs and Fe-CDs. (d) XPS spectrum of Fe-CDs. High resolution of (e) C 1s; (f) O 1s; (g) N 1s; (h) Cl 2p; and (i) Fe 2p binding energy.

Michaelis‒Menten curves of Fe-CDs with (a) H2O2 and (c) OPD. The Lineweaver‒Burk linear relationship of 1/V and 1/[S] for the reaction of Fe-CDs with (b) H2O2 and (d) OPD double-reciprocal diagram of Fe-CDs.

(a) The absorbance at 420 nm of Fe-CDs/H2O2/OPD with various concentrations of different ROS scavengers. (b) Detection of ·OH in different NE concentration systems.(c) Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanism for OPD oxidation by H2O2 under Fe-CDs catalysis.

(a) Model of Fe-CDs. (b) Optimized structures of H2O2 ∗, HO2
. ∗, and H2O + ·OH ∗ intermediates adsorbed on Fe-CDs. (c) Calculated free energy changes from H2O2 to ·OH on Fe-CDs.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 at different concentrations of (a) NE; (b) AD; and (c) DA. The linear relationship between the absorbance and (d) NE; (e) AD; and (f) DA.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of DA interaction with each component of the Fe-CDs-based nano-enzyme detection system. (b) Fluorescence spectra of DA interaction with each component of the Fe-CDs-based nano-enzyme detection system. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of DA and Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 systems and excitation fluorescence spectrum of Fe-CDs. (d) Possible reaction mechanism of DA and Fe-CDs.

Two-dimensional PCA plots of the sensor array for discrimination of CAs with concentrations at (a) 5 μM; (b)10 μM; and (c) 50 μM. Heat map of different concentrations of (d) 5 μM; (e)10 μM; and (f) 50 μM. Binary mixture analysis of CAs with concentrations at (g) 5 μM; (h)10 μM; and (i) 50 μM.
Binary mixtures analysis of CAs with concentrations at (a) 5 μM; (b) 10 μM; and (c) 50 μM. (d) Ternary mixtures analysis of CAs with concentrations at 10 μM.

The absorption for the mixture of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 with different substances (a) A/A0 at 420 nm; (b) A350/A420; (c) The UV–vis spectra of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 with various substances; The absorption for the mixture of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 containing different substances (d) with/without NE; (e) with/without AD; (f) with/without DA.

The absorption of three batches of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 (a) with NE; (b) with DA; Comparison between the different batches of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 for the detection of (c) NE; (d) DA; (e) The UV–vis spectra of Fe-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) synthesized from different batches. (f) The absorption of Fe-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) at 420 nm in Water for 20 days.
(a) Schematic representation of CAs detection using a smartphone. Linear relationships between B/R and (b) NE; (c) AD; and (d) DA concentrations.

(a) Scheme for the smartphone-assisted determination of CAs in urine and fetal bovine serum. B/R values of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 responses to (b) NE; (c) AD; and (d) DA in real samples by using a smartphone.
Schematic illustrations of Fe-CDs-based colorimetric sensor array for identification of CAs.
Quantification of CAs in the urine samples with RSD (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND∗: Below the detection limit, not detected

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantification of CAs in the urine samples with RSD using smartphone.

head.ce:title: Single-well colorimetric sensor array for discrimination and smartphone-assisted detection of catecholamines based on Fe-carbon dots nanozymes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education), Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology, National “111” Center for Cellular Regulation and Molecular Pharmaceutics, School of Life and Health Sciences, Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan, 430068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education)
Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology
National “111” Center for Cellular Regulation and Molecular Pharmaceutics
School of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education), Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology, National “111” Center for Cellular Regulation and Molecular Pharmaceutics, School of Life and Health Sciences, Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan, 430068, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Catecholamines (CAs), such as noradrenaline (NE), adrenaline (AD), and dopamine (DA), are essential signaling mediators that regulate various physiological functions. Monitoring their levels is crucial for studying and diagnosing diseases, as abnormal concentrations are associated with numerous health conditions. However, distinguishing between these CAs is challenging due to their highly similar molecular structures.

In this study, Fe-doped carbon dot-based nanozymes (Fe-CDs) with strong peroxidase-like activity were synthesized using a simple one-pot method. Fe-CDs-based sensing systems exhibit excellent stability, reproducibility, sensitivity (with detection limits of 26.6 nM for NE, 46.0 nM for AD, and 33.3 nM for DA), and anti-interference properties. A triple-channel single-well colorimetric sensor array was developed by collecting the absorbance at 20, 40, and 60 min as sensing units, enabling the effective differentiation and identification of various CAs.

The Fe-CDs-based system has proven capable of detecting CAs in real human urine and fetal bovine serum. Additionally, the Fe-CDs-based smartphone-assisted platform provides efficient, highly sensitive, and on-site CAs detection, making it highly promising for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Catecholamines (CAs) such as norepinephrine (NE), adrenaline (AD), and dopamine (DA) play crucial roles in regulating the physiological functions of cardiovascular, respiratory, metabolic, and immune systems. However, abnormal levels of CAs can severely disrupt the balance of physiological systems, leading to significant changes in pulmonary, coronary artery, and cerebral vascular functions, and increasing risks of myocardial infarction, stroke, hypertension, arrhythmias, thrombosis, and diabetes [1,2]. It has been shown that CAs are also closely related to the development of cancer. Monitoring CAs levels not only reflects their metabolic status in the body, which is helpful for early warning of thyroid dysfunction, hypertension, adrenal medullary hyperplasia, and other diseases, but also has important clinical significance for the early diagnosis and differentiation of the central nervous system disorders such as pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma (PHEO/PGL) and neurodegenerative diseases and so on [3,4]. Therefore, tracking the concentrations of these CAs as biomarkers represents a promising strategy for the early detection and treatment of these serious diseases. At present, the main methods for detecting CAs concentration are high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and electrochemistry, known for their high sensitivity and accuracy [5]. However, HPLC requires bulky, costly specialized instruments, intricate pre-processing procedures, and highly trained operators, leading to significant expenses and time-consuming [6,7]. Electrochemical analysis, though sensitive, often faces challenges with selectivity and reproducibility in neurotransmitter detection [8]. In recent years, spectrophotometric techniques have gained traction for CAs detection in organisms. Compared to fluorescence spectroscopy, colorimetric detection stands out for its simplicity and cost-effectiveness, not relying on specialized light sources for excitation, making it a more affordable and straightforward method for intuitive detection [9–13]. However, conventional colorimetric methods are unable to fully distinguish between DA, NE, and AD, and struggle to accurately quantify individual CAs. Given the practical demands, it is crucial to develop a straightforward and sensitive method capable of both detecting and identifying CAs [14,15]. In recent years, colorimetric sensor array technology has provided the possibility to solve this challenge [16–18]. Inspired by mammalian taste or smell systems, array sensing methods enable simultaneous determination and identification of multiple analytes with similar structural or chemical characteristics [19,20]. These sensing arrays consist of specific receptor-like “key” colorimetric sensors and cross-responsive sensing elements, generating distinct compound response patterns (fingerprints) for each target analyte [21–23]. This approach is versatile for detecting and recognizing various targets [24–26]. To date, some studies have employed colorimetric sensor arrays for CAs detection. However, these multi-material colorimetric arrays often involved three or more components, increasing experimental complexity, raw material consumption, and multi-sample consumption ratio discrepancy [10,12,27,28] (see Scheme 1).

Nanozymes are emerging nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity, offering greater stability, cost-effectiveness, and ease of preparation compared to natural enzymes. Among various nanozymes, carbon dot-based nanozymes stand out due to their small size, high surface area, excellent stability, outstanding biocompatibility, versatile catalytic activity, and favorable optical properties. Additionally, they are easy to synthesize and functionalize, and are environmentally friendly [29–32]. In recent years, the doping of carbon dots (CDs) with various elements, particularly metals, has significantly enhanced their enzyme-mimetic activities. The variable valence states and enhanced electron transfer capabilities of these metals enable superior peroxidase-like activities. These attributes make carbon dot-based nanozymes particularly appealing for diverse applications, including biosensing, medical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring [33–36].

In this work, Fe-doped carbon dot-based nanozymes (Fe-CDs) were synthesized via a one-pot method for the detection of CAs. These nanozymes exhibit strong peroxidase-like activity, excellent stability, and reproducibility. To improve upon the limitations of multi-material colorimetric arrays, a triple-channel sensor array was developed using a Fe-CDs-based single-well sensing system, with different reaction times (20, 40, 60 min) as sensing units ( Scheme 1) [37,38]. This sensor array effectively differentiates CAs at various concentrations by utilizing the varying activities of the nanozymes and their distinct effects on the chromogenic product 2,3-diamino phenazine (oxidized OPD, oxOPD). Furthermore, the Fe-CDs-based sensing system was capable of detecting CAs in real samples, such as human urine and fetal bovine serum, underscoring its potential for disease diagnosis. The integration of Fe-CDs with a smartphone-assisted platform enables efficient, highly sensitive, and on-site CAs detection. Thus, Fe-CDs-based sensing systems offer significant promise for applications in biomedicine and disease diagnosis.

citric acid (5 mmol, 3.84 g), urea (30 mmol, 7.27 g), FeCl3·6H2O (2.5 mmol, 2.73 g), and Sudan I (0.5 mmol, 0.49 g) were dissolved in DMF (40 mL) and then sonicated for 20 min at room temperature. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. When the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature, and the supernatant was collected by centrifugation (10 min/10000 rpm). To the supernatant 20 mL ethanol was added to obtain precipitate, which was washed with ethanol three times. The resulting black powder Fe-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

A colorimetric detection method for CAs was established in the Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 reaction system. Typically, 0.5 mL H2O2 solution (5 mM) was added to a mixture containing various concentrations of CAs, along with 1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL Fe-CDs and 0.5 mL OPD (1.5 mM). The reaction proceeded for 120 min at room temperature in darkness to ensure complete catalysis of H2O2 and prevent light-induced interference. We recorded the UV spectra of the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembling a triple-channel single-well sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and sensitivity of the Fe-CDs-based triple-channel single-well array sensor depend on varying activities of nanozymes at different reaction times and the effects of different CAs on oxOPD. Consequently, responses of Fe-CDs-based sensing systems at 20, 40, and 60 min were chosen as the sensing unit for the colorimetric sensor arrays for CAs detection and identification. Based on this, a triple-channel single-well array sensor colorimetric sensor array (3 CAs × 3 receptor units × 5 replicates) was developed. To further validate the differentiation ability of Fe-CDs-based sensor array to CAs, different concentrations of single and binary CAs were analyzed. SPSS 27.0 software was used for PCA processing of the raw matrix data.

The urine samples were gathered from healthy volunteers after obtaining informed consent. The samples underwent clarification via filtration through a 0.22 μm Millipore membrane to eliminate impurities and particulate matter. Subsequently, the urine filtrate was diluted with water for further analysis.1.0 mL of fetal bovine serum (FBS) was mixed with 3.0 mL of methanol, shaken for 1 min, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove proteins. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and diluted as serum filtrate.

The test samples were prepared by adding 40 μL of standard solutions of DA (dissolved in water), NE (dissolved in DMSO), or AD (dissolved in DMSO) at various concentrations to 2 mL of the previously prepared biological filtrate. Each biological test sample (20 μL) was then mixed with 1 mL of Fe-CDs stock solution (100 μg/mL), 0.5 mL of OPD (1.5 mM), and 0.5 mL of H2O2 (5 mM) and incubated for 2 h. Concentration analysis of CAs was performed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer or smartphone-assisted point-of-care testing (POCT). For HPLC analysis, the column temperature was 40 °C, the flow rate was 600 μL/min, mobile phase A was water (0.1 % formic acid), mobile phase B was acetonitrile (0.1 % formic acid), and the gradient elution procedure was 0 min, 5 % phase B; 2.5 min, 50 % phase B; 3.0 min, 95 % phase B: 3.5 min, 5 % phase B; 10.0 min, 5 % phase B. The linear standard curve was established with five concentration levels (NE, AD: 1, 2.5, 5, 7 μM; DA: 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 μM; Fig. S1).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that Fe-CDs are nearly spherical morphologies with excellent dispersion. The particle size distribution ranged from 1.07 to 7.72 nm, with an average diameter of 3.31 nm (Fig. 1 a). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Fig. 1b, Fig. S2a and Fig. S2b) indicated well-dispersed Fe-CDs with heights ranging from 1.16 to 2.62 nm, suggests that Fe-CDs comprise approximately 4–9 graphene layers. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) (Fig. 1c) was used to identify the surface functional groups on Fe-CDs. The broadband absorption peak at 3435 cm−1 is attributed to N–H/O–H stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 3167 cm−1 is related to the C–H bending vibration. The peak at 1622 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching vibration. The absorption center at 1374 cm−1 is the C–N stretching vibration peak. The peak at 1061 cm−1 may be related to the C–O bending vibration. In addition, the typical characteristic peak of 639 cm−1 corresponds to Fe–O bonds. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Fe-CDs (Fig. S2d) exhibited major absorption peaks around 200–250 nm, 300–350 nm, 400–450 nm, and 530–600 nm. These peaks can be attributed to the π → π∗ transitions of CC bonds in the carbon matrix, n → π∗ transitions of CO groups, and absorption edges induced by surface molecular centers or n → π∗ transitions of non-bonding electrons of atoms. Additionally, the peak at 550 nm corresponds to the n → π∗ transition of the π-system containing C–N/CN. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S2e) of Fe-CDs shows a typical 2θ peak at 26.8°, which is attributed to the (002) graphitic carbon diffraction. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the elemental composition and chemical bonding of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1d), with peaks observed at 711.57 eV (Fe 2p), 531.16 eV (O 1s), 399.64 eV (N 1s), 284.48 eV (C 1s), and 190.42 eV (Cl 2p). In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e), the peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.06 eV, 287.75 eV, and 289.74 eV correspond to the binding energies of C–C, C–N/C–O, C–Cl/C–OH, and CN/CO bonds, respectively. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), the peaks at 531.54 eV, 530.92 eV, and 532.09 eV indicate the presence of C–O, CO, and Fe–O compositions, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) exhibits peaks at 398.88, 401.49, and 400.00 eV, confirming C–N–C, C–NH2, and CN–C binding energies for different nitrogen groups. The peaks at 198.20 and 200.00 eV in the Cl 2p spectrum (Fig. 1h) are attributed to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1i) displays prominent peaks at 724.2 eV (Fe3+ 2p3/2) and 711.8 eV (Fe2+ 2p3/2), with additional peaks at 716.8 eV (Fe3+ 2p1/2) and 728.4 eV (Fe2+ 2p1/2). Therefore, the detailed XPS analysis of Fe-CDs showed specific peaks corresponding to C–C/C–N/CN/CO/C–Cl/C–OH bonds, indicating the surface chemistry and oxidation states of Fe within the carbon dots.

The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs was studied using OPD as a chromogenic substrate. When only OPD or OPD + H2O2 were present in the reaction system, the absorbance of the system at 420 nm was essentially zero. This indicated that there was no catalytic activity of Fe-CDs, the substrate OPD itself did not show color, and it could not be oxidized to show color in the presence of H2O2. Therefore, there was no change in absorbance at 420 nm. When Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs + OPD, or Fe-CDs + H2O2 were present in the reaction system, it was found that the overall absorbance was shifted upwards, but the magnitude of the change was not significant. This was attributed to the intrinsic black color of the nanomaterials themselves. However, when both Fe-CDs + H2O2 + OPD were present in the system, there was a noticeable increase in absorbance at 420 nm. This suggested that Fe-CDs catalyzed the oxidation of H2O2, leading to the conversion of OPD to oxOPD (Fig. S2f).

To systematically assess the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs, steady-state kinetic analysis was conducted using OPD as the chromogenic substrate in the presence of the oxidant H2O2. Kinetic parameters K m and V max were determined using the Lineweaver-Burk plot, a double reciprocal transformation of the Michaelis-Menten equation. Equation (1) represents the Michaelis-Menten equation, while Equation (2) denotes the Lineweaver-Burk equation, where V and V max refer to the initial and maximum reaction rates, K
m is the Michaelis-Menten constant, and [S] denotes the substrate concentration.
(1)
V =
V max
×
[ S ]
K m
+
[ S ]
(2)
1 V
=
(
K m
V
m
a x
)
×
(
1
[ S ]
)
+
(
1
V
m
a x
)

As shown in Fig. 2 a–d, the results demonstrated that the enzymatic kinetics for both H2O2 and OPD followed the Michaelis-Menten model (Equation (1)). The K m value for H2O2 was 0.12 mM, while for OPD, it was 0.07 mM. Additionally, a Lineweaver-Burk plot (Equation (2)) was used to visualize the linear relationship between 1/V and 1/[S]. Comparative analysis revealed that Fe-CDs exhibited superior peroxidase-like activity relative to other reported enzymes (Table S1).

To elucidate the peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of Fe-CDs in the presence of H2O2, the types of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during the catalytic process were analyzed using both scavenger assays and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. Thiourea (TU), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and histidine (His) were employed as ROS scavengers to identify hydroxyl radical (·OH), superoxide anion radical (·O2 −), and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. According to the accepted view, the absorbance of the catalytic system Fe-CDs/H2O2 with OPD should decrease when specific radicals are quenched by the corresponding scavenger. The experimental results revealed an obvious decrease in absorbance at 420 nm in the presence of TU (Fig. 3 a), while the absorbance remained almost unchanged with the addition of other two scavengers. This indicates that Fe-CDs catalyze the formation of ·OH and specifically interact with TU. Additionally, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum was used to further confirm the generation of ·OH during the catalytic process of Fe-CDs in the presence of H2O2. The Fe-CDs/H2O2/DMPO system produced a spectrum with four distinct strong peaks (Fig. 3b, Fig. S4), with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, confirming the presence of ·OH.

Iron (Fe) is a key transition metal ion in Fenton reactions, where it promotes the reduction of H2O2 to ·OH. The Fenton reaction cycle involves various oxidation states of Fe cations, such as Fe3+/Fe2+, and the d-orbitals of Fe3+. Thus, a proposed mechanism suggests that in the presence of Fe-CDs, the oxidation of OPD is thought to proceed via a Fenton-like reaction, as described in Fig. 3c. The Fe-CDs enzyme efficiently decomposes H2O2 into ·OH, which then facilitates the oxidation of OPD in the Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 system at room temperature. XPS was conducted to elucidate the chemical valence status of Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs + H2O2, Fe-CDs + H2O2 + NE, Fe-CDs + H2O2 + DA (Fig. S5). Compared with Fe-CDs, Fe3+ ratio increased and Fe2+ ratio decreased after adding H2O2, which indicates that Fe2+ is partially oxidized to Fe3+. In the presence of the CAs, the absorbance peak at 420 nm decreased, indicating a reduction in the content of oxOPD. We infer that this is because CAs consumes ·OH, resulting in a decrease in the amount of ·OH involved in the oxidation of OPD. Furthermore, the reduction of ·OH was verified by EPR. As shown in Fig. 3b, when the concentration of NE increases, the content of ·OH decreases successively.

In this study, the POD-like catalytic mechanism of Fe-CDs was revealed by a density-functional theory (DFT) computational system. Based on the ORCA quantum chemistry program [39], the geometrical structure model of Fe-CDs (Fig. 4 a) was first constructed and the complete reaction pathway of their catalytic conversion of H2O2 to ·OH was simulated. Theoretical calculations show that the catalytic process follows the Fe-induced Fenton-like reaction mechanism: the H2O2 molecule is preferentially adsorbed on the Fe active site (2H2O2∗), undergoes O–H bond breaking to form the intermediate (HO2·∗), and finally generates the product (H2O + ·OH∗) through stepwise conversion. The stable adsorption configurations of each intermediate on the surface of Fe-CDs were verified by geometric optimization (Fig. 4b), and the free energy analysis of their reaction pathways (Fig. 4c) confirmed that the Fe sites effectively activated H2O2 through electron transfer to drive the efficient generation of reactive ·OH, which elucidated the electronic structural nature and kinetic mechanism of the catalytic activity of Fe-CDs-like enzymes at the atomic scale.

As expected, the absorbance of the Fe-CDs/H2O2/OPD system at 420 nm decreased with increasing concentrations of NE and AD (Fig. 5 a and b). The absorbance at 420 nm decreased in direct proportion to their concentrations. For NE, in the range of 0.5–15 μM, the regression equation is y = 0.0536x + 1.1601 (R 2 = 0.994) (Fig. 5d), with a detection limit of 26.6 nM (LOD = 3σ/k). In the range of 15–100 μM, the regression equation is y = 0.0226x + 1.6362 (R 2 = 0.995). For AD, in the range of 0.25–10 μM, the regression equation is y = 0.0429x + 1.0128(R 2 = 0.996) (Fig. 5e), with a detection limit of 33.3 nM. In the range of 10–100 μM, the regression equation is y = 0.0253x + 1.2351 (R 2 = 0.993). However, as the DA concentration increased, the absorbance at 420 nm decreased, while the absorbance value at 350 nm increased (Fig. 5c). As shown in Fig. 5f, the test system's absorbance is proportional to DA concentration in the 5–30 μM range, demonstrating good linearity. The linear regression equation is y = 0.0308x + 0.2536 (R 2 = 996), with a detection limit of 46.0 nM. For concentrations between 30 and 100 μM, the regression equation is y = 0.0217x + 0.5973 (R 2 = 0.997). Compared to most reported methods, this colorimetric strategy offers higher sensitivity (Table S2).

To elucidate the mechanism of colorimetric absorbance changes in the DA detection system, we examined the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6 a) and fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 6b) of the interaction of DA with each component of the Fe-CDs-based nanozyme detection system. The UV–Vis spectra showed that individual H2O2, Fe-CDs, the chromogenic substrate OPD, and the binary mixtures of “H2O2 + OPD” and “Fe-CDs + OPD” did not cause any significant changes in the absorbance spectra of DA solution. Only the binary mixture of “H2O2 + Fe-CDs” resulted in a strong absorption peak at 490 nm in the DA solution, which may be attributed to the formation of polydopamine (PDA) catalyzed by Fe-CDs [40]. The strong absorption peak at 420 nm in the ternary mixture solution of “Fe-CDs + H2O2 + OPD” is due to the formation of oxOPD catalyzed by Fe-CDs (Fig. 6a) In the quaternary mixed solution of “DA + Fe-CDs + H2O2 + OPD”, a strong absorption peak mainly occurred at 350 nm, which might involve the oxidation of DA to quinone under the catalysis of Fe-CDs, cyclization to form 5,6-dihydroxyindole (DHI) and 1H-pyrrolo[2,3-b]phenazine (HPP), which was then oxidized by H2O2 to form 1H-indole-5,6-dione (HID), and subsequently reacted with OPD to form 1H-pyrrolo[2,3-b]phenazine (HPP) (Fig. 6d). Finally, the oligomer CPDH formed by the copolymerization of HID and HPP led to the observed spectra absorption [41,42].

The fluorescence spectra after the interaction of DA with each component of the Fe-CDs-based enzyme detection system also support the hypothesis that DA was catalytically oxidized by Fe-CDs and polymerized with OPD to form CPDH (Fig. 6b). The binary mixtures of “DA + H2O2” and “DA + OPD,” as well as the ternary mixture “DA + H2O2 + OPD”, did not exhibit fluorescence emission. Fe-CDs showed a certain intensity of fluorescence emission at 465 nm, which increased after the addition of DA. This increase might be due to the fluorescence of the Fe-CDs, which lose iron ions after being chelated and removed by the catechol fragment of dopamine, experiencing a certain degree of recovery [43–45]. The fluorescence intensity of the ternary mixture “DA + Fe-CDs + OPD” was slightly lower than that of the binary mixture “DA + Fe-CDs,” possibly due to the high concentration of OPD partially quenching the fluorescence of this binary mixture. The fluorescence intensity of the ternary mixture “DA + Fe-CDs + H2O2” is nearly equal to that of the Fe-CDs solution, which may be due to DA being converted into a quinonoid structure HID that could not chelate Fe3+ under the catalytic effect of Fe-CDs, thus having minimal effect on the structural components and fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the quaternary mixture “DA + Fe-CDs + H2O2 + OPD” being lower than that of the Fe-CDs solution could be due to the high overlap of the absorption spectrum of CPHH generated in this quaternary mixture with the excitation spectra spectrum of the fluorescent emitting substance Fe-CDs in the quaternary mixture, leading to an inner filter effect (IFE) that partially quenched the fluorescence of Fe-CDs (Fig. 6c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Fe-CDs colorimetric sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To enable the discriminative detection of CAs with similar molecular structures and response signals, we constructed a triple-channel colorimetric sensing array by assembling absorption data collected at three different time points (20, 40, 60 min) as sensing units using a Fe-CDs-based single-well sensing system. Although the three CAs studied—NE, AD, and DA—were found to reduce the color intensity of the Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 system upon addition, further examination of the actual color changes revealed distinct visual patterns for CAs. To minimize experimental variability and ensure accurate results, five parallel samples were prepared for discriminating NE, AD and DA (target analytes) using the enzyme sensor array (20, 40, 60 min). Due to variations in time and concentration, the absorbance of the CAs differed. Utilizing OPD as the catalytic substrate, the absorbance of the sensing units exhibited varying colorimetric response patterns before and after the addition of CAs in the presence of H2O2, yielding a data matrix consisting of “3 sensing units × 3 target analytes × 5 parallel samples”, which generated a unique “fingerprint.” The transformation of complex training data matrices into two-dimensional principal component analysis (PCA) visualization maps through the application of principal component analysis. The nanozyme colorimetric sensor array (20, 40, 60 min) successfully distinguished three concentrations (5, 10, 50 μM) of CAs (Fig. 7 a–f). The PCA score plot shows that CAs of the same type are clustered together, while different CAs are distinctly separated with no misclassification, achieving 100 % accuracy at concentrations of 5, 10, and 50 μM. These results confirm that CAs are fully identifiable even at concentrations as low as 5 μM. In addition, in order to verify the analysis and discrimination ability of the established enzyme colorimetric sensor array (20, 40, 60 min) for multiple mixtures, we tested binary mixtures of the three CAs (DA, NE, AD) at various ratios: DA: NE = 50 %: 50 %, DA: AD = 50 %: 50 %, NE: AD = 50 %: 50 %, DA: NE = 15 %: 85 %, DA: NE = 10 %: 90 %, DA: AD = 25 %: 75 %, DA: AD = 15 %: 85 %, DA: AD = 10 %:90 %, NE: AD = 25 %: 75 %, NE: AD = 15 %: 85 %, and NE: AD = 10 %:90 %, with total concentrations of 5, 10, and 50 μM. As shown in Fig. 8 a–d, Binary and ternary mixture were accurately identified with misclassification, demonstrating the enzyme sensor array's exceptional performance in distinguishing standard CAs mixtures and providing a reliable foundation for detecting these CAs in real biological samples.

The selectivity and anti-interference capability are all important criteria for evaluating the excellence of a sensing system. To assess these attributes, we tested the response of Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 system toward some potential interferents (100 μM), including ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+), amino acids (Lys, Trp, Phe, Met, Thr, Ile, Leu, Val, His, Gly, Cys, Tyr), and some biomolecules (ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), 5-hydroxy indole acetic acid (5-HIAA), glucose, sucrose, fructose). As shown in Fig. S6, the addition of various CAs significantly reduces the UV absorption of the detection system Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 at 420 nm, while the addition of DA introduces a new absorption peak at 350 nm. In contrast, those potential interferents do not obviously alter the UV absorption at 420 nm (Fig. 9 a and b). Furthermore, even in the presence of these interferents, the response of the nanoenzyme sensing system to various CAs remains largely unaffected (Fig. 9c–e). The results demonstrated that none of these potential interferents significantly impacted the detection of AD, NE, and DA, indicating that the Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 platform exhibits good selectivity and anti-interference capabilities for sensing these CAs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, it is essential to account for potential interference factors such as batch-to-batch variations in Fe-CDs preparation, as well as the effects of different storage periods on the stability and accuracy of the sensing system. These factors should be thoroughly addressed in sensor system design and validated through experimental testing to ensure reliability and stability. Therefore, we assessed the reproducibility of Fe-CDs reactions using three different lots (Fig. 10 a and b). Despite similar structures and quenching behaviors, NE and AD only reduced absorbance at 420 nm, whereas DA increased absorbance at 350 nm and decreased it at 420 nm. Therefore, the responses of three different batches to NE and DA were compared using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) to assess inter-batch differences. Significant differences were further analyzed using the Student-Newman-Keuls (SNK) post hoc test, with a significance level set at p < 0.05. These results indicate that the sensing platform has good accuracy and repeatability. (Fig. 10c and d). UV spectra from 200 nm to 1000 nm showed consistent results across the three Fe-CDs batches, further demonstrating their reproducibility (Fig. 10e). Additionally, as shown in Fig. 10f, we investigated the effects of storage time on the stability of the Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 system. The results demonstrated that the catalytic activity of the sensing system remained robust even after 20 days of storage, indicating that the sensing platform has good stability.

RGB patterns are a widely used modern chromaticity analysis method that minimizes errors from human color perception. This technique finds applications in environmental monitoring, material analysis, precision agriculture, and more. To detect NE, AD, and DA using RGB patterns, a Fe-CDs/OPD/H2O2 mixture with varying analyte concentrations was added dropwise to a round titration plate. Photographs were taken with a smartphone, and this process was repeated three times without background correction. The RGB values of these images were recorded and analyzed using a free RGB color value app on the smartphone. As shown in Fig. 11 , the B/R values demonstrated a good linear correlation with analyte concentrations (y = 0.0177x + 0.1595, R 2 = 0.9901 for AD; y = 0.0229x + 0.1474, R 2 = 0.9979 for NE; y = 0.0148x + 0.2370, R 2 = 0.9922 for DA) in the 1–30 μM range. The LOD values for AD, NE, and DA were 80.8, 62.5, and 96.7 nM, respectively, confirming that the smartphone-assisted sensing platform enables efficient and highly sensitive detection of CAs.

To further validate the reliability and feasibility of Fe-CDs, we used this probe to detect CAs in real samples. As shown in Table 1 and Table S3, the recoveries in human urine and fetal bovine serum were 92.05–107.05 % and 90.34–107.46 %, respectively, with RSD values less than 5 %. In addition, the smartphone combined with RGB values was used for the quantitative detection of CAs, and quantitative analysis was performed on real biological samples using the standard addition method (Fig. 12 ). As shown in Table 2 and Table S4, the recoveries in human urine and fetal bovine serum were 96.75–102.21 % and 96.66–106.02 %, respectively, with RSD values less than 3.81 % and 4.44 %. Meanwhile, the content of catecholamines in the test samples was determined by HPLC, which was in agreement with the results of UV analysis (Table 1 and Table S3). This demonstrates that the probe is reliable for the quantitative detection of CAs in human urine and fetal bovine serum.

In this study, we synthesized Fe-CDs using a one-pot hydrothermal method, achieving materials with outstanding peroxidase-like activity. Then, we developed a triple-channel colorimetric sensor array by collecting absorption data at three time points (20, 40, 60 min) within a single Fe-CDs-based well system as sensing units. This single-well sensor array effectively distinguished CAs mixtures and reliably detected CAs. The Fe-CDs-based system was successfully applied to detect CAs in human urine and fetal bovine serum. The smartphone-assisted platform enabled efficient, highly sensitive, and on-site CAs detection. This research highlights a promising biosensor for point-of-care diagnosis of hyperecatecholaminemia.

Guang-Ming Bao: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Dan-Dan Chen: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yi-Fan Xia: Supervision. Zhi-Qiang Cai: Supervision. Shun-Qiang Cui: Supervision. Xia Wei: Supervision. Zhen-Chong Dou: Supervision. Yuan Yuan: Supervision. Akimana Sandra: Supervision. Hou-Qun Yuan: Supervision.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22166018, 22165012, 31960720), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei University of Technology (No. XJ2022007801), and the Open Project Funding of the Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education) (No. 202409FE20).
